planting.note: Plant tubers just below the soil surface in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade; African cyclamen flowers in autumn before or with the leaves and goes summer dormant. It is similar to C. hederifolium but larger in all parts — larger tuber, larger leaves, larger flowers — and slightly less cold-hardy. In zone 9 it can remain in the ground year-round; protect the tuber from hard frost in colder zones. The large tubers can reach 12 inches across in very old plants.

directions: Plant tubers near the soil surface in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part to full shade; African cyclamen is essentially a larger, more tender version of the familiar C. hederifolium — producing in autumn (September–November) before or with the leaves, pink to deep rose-pink, reflexed-petaled cyclamen flowers of the typical shuttlecock form, fragrant, and the characteristic ivy-shaped, attractively marbled and silver-patterned leaves that follow and persist through winter and spring before the plant goes summer dormant. What distinguishes C. africanum is scale: the flowers are larger than those of C. hederifolium, the marbled, silver-zoned leaves are larger, and the tubers of old plants can reach extraordinary dimensions — dinner plate-sized, flat-topped, brownish-gray corms that have been in the ground for decades and represent some of the most massive of all hardy bulbs. Native to the woodlands and scrubland of Algeria and Tunisia, it is marginally less cold-hardy than C. hederifolium, thriving in zones 8–11 and making it an outstanding candidate for the Gulf Coast and California shade garden where C. hederifolium can be inconsistent. Outstanding under the dry shade of large trees in zones 8–11.

planting.note: Plant tubers just below the soil surface with the flat root-side down in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty or rocky soil in full sun to part shade; Greek cyclamen is unusual among hardy cyclamen in tolerating full sun in Mediterranean climates and developing long, thick anchoring roots that contract deeply into the soil — in rocky cliff habitats it may be essentially un-extractable. It flowers in early autumn (September–October) before the leaves. The tuber base produces downward-pointing roots from the center and lower surfaces, unlike C. hederifolium which roots from the sides.

directions: Plant tubers in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in full sun to part shade; Greek cyclamen is the most heat- and drought-tolerant of the autumn-flowering cyclamen species — native to the rocky limestone cliffs, stony garigue, and thin, dry, alkaline soils of Greece, Crete, Turkey, and Cyprus — and therefore an outstanding cyclamen for Mediterranean-climate and warm-winter gardens in zones 7–11 where conditions are too hot and dry for C. hederifolium to thrive consistently. It flowers in early autumn (September–October) producing the reflexed-petaled, pink to rosy-pink or occasionally white shuttlecock cyclamen flowers, often with a magenta marking at the base of each petal, before the leaves unfurl. The leaves, appearing after the flowers, are exquisitely beautiful — dark green, often velvety in texture, with intricate silver-gray zoning and patterning in an infinite variety of marbling patterns, each leaf uniquely marked. It develops thick anchoring roots that pull the tuber deep into rock crevices in nature; in garden conditions the tubers build slowly to impressive size. A specialist plant for collectors and Mediterranean rock gardeners, providing both autumn flowers and outstanding winter foliage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; church steeple lily is a tropical West African species adapted to shade and moisture — growing naturally in the humid, shaded understory of tropical West African forests. In zones 9b–11 it grows well outdoors in part shade year-round; in zone 9a it benefits from protection or a sheltered, south-facing position. The common name 'church steeple lily' refers to the tall, slender, pointed buds before opening. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; church steeple lily is a shade-loving tropical West African crinum — one of the few crinum species that prefers shade rather than sun — producing in summer and autumn umbels of 6–10 pure-white, long-tubed, strongly fragrant, funnel-shaped flowers with very long perianth tubes that taper to a point in bud before opening (the 'church steeple' shape that gives the common name). The opened flowers are pure white with a very slender tube and spreading lobes, sweetly fragrant in the evening, and elegant rather than bold in character — a refined white tropical lily for the shade garden. Native to the humid, shaded understory of West and Central African tropical forests from Senegal to Uganda, it evolved for consistent shade and moisture, making it an invaluable crinum for the zone 9–11 tropical shade garden where few other large summer-flowering bulbs with genuine shade tolerance are available. Combined with clivias, drimiopsis, and other shade-tolerant South African and tropical bulbs, it provides an extended season of white fragrant flowers in the deep shade garden. The dark-green, broad strap-shaped leaves are handsome throughout the year.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall or transplant container-grown plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; fern-leaf corydalis is nearly evergreen in mild climates, providing attractive ferny foliage year-round. It self-sows freely in suitable moist, shaded conditions. Unlike the blue and yellow perennial corydalis species, this one blooms in spring and early summer, producing dense, upright racemes of golden-yellow tubular flowers above the fine, fern-like leaves.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; fern-leaf corydalis is a Chinese woodland corydalis that provides two distinct garden qualities in one compact plant: extraordinary foliage and good spring flowers. The foliage is the main attraction — finely divided, multi-pinnate, lacy leaves of extreme delicacy, closely resembling the fronds of certain ferns or the foliage of fine ferny rock garden plants, in a soft, bronze-tinted green that deepens to bronzy-red in cooler weather and is nearly evergreen in mild climates, providing attractive winter presence in the shade garden. Against this cloud of delicate ferny foliage, dense, upright, cylindrical racemes of small, spurred, golden-yellow tubular flowers appear in spring and early summer — the soft yellow blooms contrasting warmly with the bronze-green fronds. It self-sows freely in cool, moist, shaded conditions, establishing naturalized colonies in suitable woodland garden soil. The combination of fern-like texture, bronze-green color, near-evergreen presence, and yellow spring flowers makes it an unusually versatile and attractive shade groundcover plant for zones 5–8.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; Siberian bugloss is larger in leaf and more vigorous than the better-known B. macrophylla, but lacks the popular silver-leaved cultivars of that species. It forms dense groundcover colonies in moist, shaded conditions and is more drought-tolerant once established than its appearance suggests. The tiny, bright-blue forget-me-not flowers appear in spring before the large leaves are fully expanded.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; Siberian bugloss is the larger and more vigorous Brunnera species — surpassing the more commonly grown B. macrophylla in leaf size (the heart-shaped, rough-textured, bright-green leaves can reach 12 inches across), vigor, and spreading capacity. In early spring, before the large leaves are fully expanded, it produces a cloud of tiny, brilliant sky-blue, five-petaled flowers identical in form to forget-me-nots (the borage-family relationship is clear) — held in loose, branching sprays above the emerging foliage. After flowering, the large, coarse-textured, heart-shaped leaves expand to form a bold, weed-smothering groundcover through the remainder of the growing season, at a scale that is more dramatic and architectural than B. macrophylla. Native to the forests and shaded river banks of Siberia and adjacent regions, it is among the hardiest of all shade perennials, surviving to zone 3, and thrives in the cool, moist, shaded conditions that suit it in zones 3–7. An excellent large-scale shade groundcover for woodland gardens where bold, weed-suppressing foliage through summer and brilliant spring blue flowers are both desired.
